shippers.name: Don Kings Ize-Iyamu
shippers.experience: It was a rollercoaster that taught me that i can't do everything on my own, my biggest challenge was working in a team.
shippers.differently: Integrate myself more deeply and socially into the team and make myself known earlier on to all the devs
shippers.bestTask: Stage 7: Q3 Roadmap Crisis: Prioritization & Stakeholder Pushback (VendSync)

shippers.name: Bassey John Godstime
shippers.experience:
I will say that HNG deliberately simulates the toughest possible version of a real work environment so that by the time you are done, you can cope anywhere.

Every stage had a cutoff mark and a deadline. I delivered or I did not move forward. 

Ten stages covering market research, technical documentation, VR product conceptualisation, ticket writing, roadmap prioritisation, and live mentor presentations. 

Each one tested a different dimension of my product thinking. The wins, the losses, the points gained, the points lost, the friends made and those lost to deactivation all made it one of the most formative experiences I have had.
shippers.differently:
If I were starting again I would be more involved in my team from day one. I would speak up earlier, ask my questions without hesitation, and invest more intentionally in building connections from the very beginning.

I made the mistake of being present but silent for too long. I had questions, ideas, and observations that I kept to myself because I was not confident enough to voice them. 

I did not realise until later that in a workspace like HNG, silence reads as absence no matter how online you are.

The relationships and the visibility matter just as much as the work itself and I would not wait until I was pushed into a leadership role to understand that.
shippers.bestTask: Stage 7: Q3 Roadmap Crisis: Prioritization & Stakeholder Pushback (VendSync)
shippers.worstTask: Stage 3: "Life as a Product" PRD & Go-To-Market Strategy
shippers.takeaway:
The biggest takeaway from CredLane is that the most consequential product decisions are not about features. They are about trust architecture.

CredLane's entire value proposition rests on one promise to employers. Every candidate you see has already been verified. The moment that promise has a single crack in it the whole platform collapses. Not gradually, but immediately.

Working on the Assessment and Scoring Engine taught me that when building a trust based product, every technical decision is also a product decision. 

The retake gate was not just an engineering constraint. It was a signal to employers that the verification meant something. The three strike integrity layer was not just an anti cheat mechanism. It was what made the Job Ready badge credible.

The takeaway I carry is to understand what my product's core promise is, identify every point where that promise could break, and protect those points before I  build anything else.
shippers.mentorsShout:
Thank you to every mentor who showed up for this cohort. The ones who reviewed our work at midnight, gave feedback that stung but was necessary, and pushed us past what we thought we were capable of.

You did not have to invest the way you did and the fact that you did means more than most of us will say out loud.

A few I have to name personally.

To Lawal
You told me from Stage 0 that I could be a finalist if I put in the work. I held onto that and it carried me further than I expected.

To Khay Umi
You never let me slip. Every time I showed up below my best you noticed and you pushed. That kind of accountability is rare and I am better for it.

To Aisha
You trusted me with team leadership before I fully trusted myself with it. That decision changed how I showed up for the rest of the internship.
shippers.next:
The internship ends but the building does not.

I am focused on deepening the technical side of my product practice and actively looking for a product role where I can work on something that genuinely matters to the African market. 

Healthcare, fintech, anything at the intersection of infrastructure and human dignity. That is where my background and my instincts point.

HNG gave me the foundation. What comes next is building on it deliberately.
shippers.advice:
HNG is not for people who lack tenacity. It is not about how much experience you have coming in. 

I watched teammates with far more product management experience than me fall off in the early stages simply because they could not commit fully to the program.

Go in knowing it will demand your time, your energy, and your mental health. Go in prepared for that cost.

Speak up from day one. Ask your questions. Challenge ideas. Build connections early. The relationships and the visibility matter just as much as the work itself.

And when it gets overwhelming, and it will, remember that the pressure is the point. Everything HNG throws at you is preparing you to cope anywhere once you are done.
Put in the work. The results will show.

shippers.name: Akanimoh John
shippers.experience: My HNG14 experience was tasking, intriguing, memorable, eye-opening and fun.
shippers.differently: Network more and be intentionally present.
shippers.bestTask: Stage 6a: Cross-Team Product Audit & Approval Review
shippers.worstTask: Stage 1: UX Audit & Bug Hunt (New User Onboarding)
shippers.takeaway:
If there's one thing HNG14 taught me that no bootcamp ever could, it's this: building with people is genuinely hard, and it's also the only way anything real gets made.
Before this internship, I thought great products came from great ideas. Spend enough time in the right headspace, map out the right solution, write a clean PRD and the rest follows. Working on EnergyIQ dismantled that belief stage by stage.
What I learned is that a Product Manager's real job is not the document. It's the room. Getting the right people pointed in the same direction, keeping them there when things get hard, and knowing when to make a call and when to listen longer. That's the job. EnergyIQ went from a struggling team near the bottom of the leaderboard to a functional product we. Not because the idea got better, but because the people got better at being a team. That shift, i.e learning to trust each other, communicate faster, cover gaps without being asked — that's what moved us.
Building alone is cleaner, faster in the short run and easier on one's ego. However, building with a team is where the actual product lives.
shippers.mentorsShout:
Big shout-out to all the amazing mentors I met during HNG14. In no particular order, I say a big thank you to:
Tearsmith, my Product Owner and the person who kept me from spiraling more than once. You were present when it mattered and honest when it was easier not to be. That combination is rarer than people admit.
Marv.lus, you reached out after a presentation I thought had tanked my confidence for good. You didn't have to. The fact that you did, and that you shared your own stumbles along the way, meant more than you know.
S.F Tommy, thank you, for your no fluff, no rush, patient, and practical approach to teaching. I vividly remember during stage 6B presentation, you guided me on appropriate ticket writing that finally made sense despite my struggle with the concept. I can't thank you enough. 
Thank you Avi for the honest reviews. The ones that made me go back and rethink, not just revise.
Nayohmee, your direct, no-nonsense, no sugarcoating guidance is exactly what I needed when I was tempted to get comfortable. Thank you for keep it real. 
Kalada, always approachable, always willing to listen. That matters more during the hard stages than anyone say out loud. Thank you for consistently being available for the team. 
Thank you Sodiqbinaliu, though your check-in scores stung. I'm still grateful. You pushed me to think harder, and I came out sharper for it.
shippers.grateful:
MiDEA of EnergyIQ
Tearsmith
Marv.lus
Nayohmee
Riri
Kalada
Avi
shippers.next: Implement what I have learnt, get a PM job and start my YouTube channel talking about PM/AI
shippers.advice: Get ready to witness a life changing experience. To get the best out of it, be teachable, ready to learn, unlearn and relearn. One rare gift you'll get is the opportunity to meet new people, do not fail to build genuine network.

architects.name: Maazi Paschal
architects.experience: My internship days were better, but, the quality of PM (finalists) in this cohort is cool. Was pretty stressful, but what's an internship without a great team?
architects.differently: Asynchronous communication. Resources or docs over long unnecessary meetings.
architects.bestTask: Stage 7: Q3 Roadmap Crisis: Prioritization & Stakeholder Pushback (VendSync)
architects.worstTask: Stage 2: Country Market Analysis & Product Variation Userflow
architects.takeaway: You don't decide what makes your product valuable, the people you serve do.
architects.favProduct: I'd have loved to have a sneak-peak on Personal Trainer, but I'm cool with Clinsight
architects.advice: Don't outsource critical thinking. That's your greatest asset. Also, be quick to get results for yourself and your product.
